result:
I want to write about the small aperture through which we view the universe, the pinhole in the cardboard box of our skulls, and how we nevertheless insist on stuffing entire galaxies through it.  
The eye is only about twenty-four millimeters across, a wet marble perched in a calcium cradle, yet it insists it can see “everything.” It can’t. It can barely see anything. It can see a sliver of the electromagnetic spectrum that we have arrogantly named “visible light,” as though the rest were somehow optional. We are like a person who walks into the Library of Congress, opens one book to page 214, sniffs the paper, and declares, “Literature examined.”

Still, that sliver is enough to make us weep. A sunset can crash a human operating system faster than any malware. We will stand on interstate medians, cars hissing by at eighty miles an hour, just to watch a burning star dip behind a Jiffy Lube sign, and we will call it beautiful even though the petroleum rainbow in the parking-lot puddle is chemically closer to the toxic runoff of our own lives than to any poem we once memorized. Beauty is not picky; it will use whatever courier is available.

I think often about the word “vertigo.” Strictly it means the sensation of spinning when you are not, but colloquially it has come to include any dizzy confrontation with scale: looking down a canyon, up at Orion, or into the gap between who we promised we would become and who we actually turned out to be. That last one is the tallest cliff. No railing. No souvenir photo. Just the wind of years rushing upward in your face, fluttering your shirt like a flag whose country no longer exists.

When I was eight I buried a time capsule behind the shed: a plastic film canister containing a Garfield sticker, a nickel, and a note that read “Do you have flying cars yet?” I spent the afternoon patting the dirt with a gravity usually reserved for cemeteries. Two decades later I returned with a trowel. The ground was softer than memory, as if it, too, had been rehearsing my return. I found nothing. Not even the canister. The earth had swallowed my parcel and refused delivery. I stood there absurdly betrayed, holding a garden tool like a detective whose prime suspect is the planet itself. Later that night I googled “how deep does a hole have to be before it becomes a grave,” then closed the laptop, ashamed of the melodrama my curiosity had ripened into.

We are all amateur archaeologists of our own premature ruins. We sift through drawer-bottom artifacts: ticket stubs, bleached concert bracelets, the orange plastic cap from a long-discarded syringe that once delivered medicine to a now-dead dog. Each object is a Rosetta Stone that deciphers only a single afternoon. We hoard them as though one day we will be put on trial and required to prove we were ever really present. “Exhibit C: a movie ticket for a film I can no longer summarize.” The jury of later selves peers over half-moon spectacles, unconvinced.

There is a neurological condition called aphantasia in which the mind’s eye is blind. Close your eyes and picture an apple: nothing. No red, no shine, no Norman-Rockwell curve. Ask the average person to imagine a beach and they get a Dolby-surround internal postcard; the aphantasic gets the printed word “beach” floating in a dark room, Times New Roman, 12-point, no illustration. I do not have aphantasia, but sometimes I envy it. Imagine—sorry, conceive of—a life without intrusive images of every mistake you ever made arriving uninvited at 2:14 a.m. in IMAX. Imagine grief without surround-sound flashbacks. Imagine a mind that stores pain as footnotes instead of frescoes. Would that feel like peace, or like being locked out of your own house?

I once read that the human heart pumps about enough blood in a lifetime to fill three oil tankers. That statistic sounds impressive until you realize it is also exactly the wrong unit of measurement. We do not experience our lives in cubic meters. We experience them in coffee spoons, in the pause before a reply text, in the moment the grocery bag splits and the oranges avalanche onto asphalt and roll in all directions like embarrassed planets. The heart is not a pump; it is a drummer who refuses to count the song off properly, starting too fast, then slowing, then missing a beat entirely when the person you love says your name in exactly the timbre you had given up hoping to ever hear again.

We keep trying to translate the heart’s work into numbers that will fit on a dashboard: steps, calories, likes, resting rate, HRV, follower count. The body obliges for a while, then quietly files an objection: a twitching eyelid, a dream about teeth crumbling like chalk. The body is a labor union the mind keeps trying to bust. One day it walks off the job entirely and we call that death, but even then the metrics fail: 0 bpm, and yet something has clearly left the building. The stadium is empty, but the echo behaves like a crowd.

I want a new unit of measurement for the distance between two people who once shared a blanket. It is not miles. It is not even silence. It is the sudden recollection that the word “goodnight” once contained an entire alphabet of inside jokes, and now is just a polite signal to hang up. That distance expands inside the chest like a balloon made of something heavier than air—lead, maybe, or the metal mercury was rumored to have been mined from. You breathe around it because you must, but every inhale rubs an edge against the sternum.

Maybe the reason we keep looking up at the night sky is not awe but homesickness. Somewhere in the mess of carbon and regret we remember that every atom heavier than hydrogen was forged in a furnace we nightly mistake for decoration. We are the universe’s way of sending a postcard to itself: “Wish you were here.” The postcard arrives folded, creased, illegible in parts, stained with coffee rings shaped like spiral galaxies. The return address is smudged, so we keep sending it upward again, affixed with a stamp that costs exactly one lifetime.

In the meantime we practice smaller renaissances: we repaint the kitchen, we learn to make sourdough, we rescue a dog who has already survived two homes and will outlive a third. We pretend these are distractions rather than notes in the same long song. The dog does not know the word “forever,” only “walk,” “treat,” “stay,” and yet manages to embody the former by repeating the latter three. If that isn’t a miracle, we have misdefined the term.

I have no tidy ending. The aperture is still small. The stars are still far. The heart is still drumming, off-tempo, in the dark. But somewhere in the film canister of the chest, a nickel glints, a sticker fades, and a question waits its whole life to be delivered to a version of me who finally has the right answer, which is probably another question.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
